title: CSR: Smart Scheduling & Appointment Triage
description: Build a schedule that keeps the clinic running smoothly: matching appointment types to time slots, recognizing what needs to be seen today, managing the flow, and handling no-shows and double-bookings.

lessons.title: Matching the Visit to the Slot

blocks.content:
Not all appointments need the same amount of time, and scheduling them well keeps the whole day from falling apart.

Typical categories (your clinic will have its own):
• Quick visits: nail trims, recheck, vaccine-only — short slots.
• Standard exams: wellness checks, minor issues — standard slots.
• Long visits: new puppy/kitten with full workup, sick patient with multiple issues, senior exams — longer slots.
• Drop-offs and surgeries: handled differently, often morning.

When booking, ask enough to gauge the visit: 'What's the main reason for the visit?' A 'he's been limping for a week and also has a lump and needs his shots' is not a quick slot. Mismatched scheduling is the #1 cause of a clinic running behind.
Always ask the reason for the visit before choosing a slot. 'Just a quick check' often turns into three problems once they're in the room.

blocks.front: Why ask the reason for a visit before booking the slot?
blocks.back: To match the appointment length to the actual need — mismatched scheduling is a top cause of the clinic running behind.

blocks.question: A client wants 'a quick appointment' but mentions the dog is limping, has a new lump, AND is due for vaccines. How should you book it?

options.feedback: Three issues need real exam time; a short slot guarantees the day runs behind.

options.text: A longer slot appropriate for multiple concerns
options.feedback: Correct — multiple issues need adequate time; book accordingly.

options.text: Tell them to only bring up one problem
options.feedback: Never limit care; schedule enough time for what the pet needs.

modules.title: Module 2 — What Needs to Be Seen Today

blocks.content:
Some things can wait two weeks; some need to be seen today; some are emergencies (covered in your Triage course). As a CSR you make scheduling-urgency judgments without diagnosing.

Generally same-day or urgent (route per protocol): not eating for more than a day, repeated vomiting/diarrhea, limping that's worsening, eye problems, possible pain, a cat not urinating (emergency).

Generally routine: annual wellness, vaccine boosters not overdue, nail trims, a lump that's been stable for months.

When unsure, err toward caution and check with the medical team about timing. 'Let me check with our team on how soon we should see her' is always a safe move.

Eye issues and 'not eating' get underestimated. Eyes can deteriorate fast, and a cat that hasn't eaten for a day-plus can develop serious liver problems. When in doubt, ask the medical team about timing.

blocks.prompt: A client says their indoor cat hasn't eaten anything in two days but is 'acting mostly normal.' How urgently do you schedule?

options.feedback: A cat not eating for two days can develop serious complications; this isn't routine.

options.text: Treat as needing prompt attention and check with the medical team on timing
options.feedback: Correct — anorexia in cats is time-sensitive; escalate the scheduling question rather than guessing.

options.text: Tell them to try different food and call back in a week
options.feedback: That's medical advice and delays needed care.

blocks.front: A cat that hasn't eaten for 2+ days — routine or prompt?
blocks.back: Prompt — feline anorexia can cause serious liver problems quickly. Check with the medical team on timing.

blocks.content:
A schedule is a living thing. Managing it well means:

• Spacing appointments realistically and protecting buffer/catch-up time.
• Confirming appointments (reminders cut no-shows dramatically).
• Having a plan for no-shows and late arrivals per clinic policy.
• Handling the inevitable double-booking or walk-in gracefully — apologize, communicate wait times honestly, and keep the medical team informed.
• Communicating delays to waiting clients before they have to ask.

Honesty about wait times builds trust. 'The doctor is running about 20 minutes behind, I'm so sorry — can I get you anything?' is far better than letting someone stew.
Proactively telling a waiting client about a delay — before they come ask — turns frustration into appreciation. People forgive waits; they don't forgive being ignored.

blocks.front: What's the best way to handle a clinic running behind schedule?
blocks.back: Proactively and honestly communicate the delay to waiting clients before they have to ask.

blocks.question: You realize two appointments were accidentally double-booked. What's the professional response?

options.feedback: Leaving it to chance risks two upset clients and a scheduling mess.

options.text: Acknowledge it, communicate honestly with both clients about timing, and coordinate with the medical team
options.feedback: Correct — own it, communicate, and problem-solve with the team.
